--- Thread (42 messages, 2018-03-04) ---
[2018-03-04 04:27] f300xen (f0x): he wants it to block immediately but not be able to attack immediately
[2018-03-04 04:27] f300xen (f0x): because the notation is good
[2018-03-04 04:27] http404error: Oh, so it comes into play exhausted
[2018-03-04 04:27] strshplyr5046 (StrShPlyr): 1Gold, 1Attack, 5 HP. Or 2Gold, 4Attack.
[2018-03-04 04:27] http404error: Wait, that doesn't work either
[2018-03-04 04:28] http404error: Admittedly the game rules are a little obtuse at times, in imagining how these kinds of cases work out
[2018-03-04 04:28] http404error: How about just not give it buildtime 2
[2018-03-04 04:28] http404error: Yes, those don't work together
[2018-03-04 04:28] iminabearsuit: There's no other unit I can think of that incentivises getting bad base set units. (Maybe something like Arka and Lancetooth). I think it might be too restrictive, and play too similarly in sets, but the concept is solid.
[2018-03-04 04:28] http404error: Prompt means "buildtime 0" whereas most units are default "buildtime 1"
[2018-03-04 04:29] punf: arms race incentivises getting bad base set units
[2018-03-04 04:29] http404error: I wouldn't call Arms Race an incentive to buy them exactly
[2018-03-04 04:29] mqp: don't think there is any game mechanic that permits unit to start blocking on turn N but not clickable until turn N+X
[2018-03-04 04:29] mqp: interesting design space though maybe
[2018-03-04 04:29] http404error: Well, most prompt units are like that where X = 1
[2018-03-04 04:29] http404error: I don't see any value in the design space of X > 1 though
[2018-03-04 04:30] http404error: It just seems needlessly complex
[2018-03-04 04:30] mqp: oh, you're right, i thought there were none like that but tia is an example
[2018-03-04 04:30] strshplyr5046 (StrShPlyr): Combine (1.3):
Supply: 4. Cost: 2, GaCannon, Splitter, Tarsier, D.
HP: 5. Prompt Blocker. Buildtime 2.
Turn: 1X. Click: 1, 3Attack.
[2018-03-04 04:30] iminabearsuit: Glad I'm not the only one that went slightly dumb on how that worked.
[2018-03-04 04:30] http404error: "clickable" is a bit of an odd condition - it's intuitive when you play it but trying to list the rules is actually challenging
[2018-03-04 04:31] iminabearsuit: It just sounds like prompt should give you the option to click it immediately, but in game it makes perfect sense.
[2018-03-04 04:31] mqp: kind of, i'm not sure it makes that much sense, i think we're just used to it
[2018-03-04 04:31] http404error: Okay, it doesn't need to say Buildtime 1, as that is the default, and also it does not have buildtime 1
[2018-03-04 04:31] iminabearsuit: Though if Apollo had prompt in the text box, a lot of players would assume it was able to shoot the turn you buy it.
[2018-03-04 04:31] mqp: i wonder if elyot learned anything about whether new players get confused about that
[2018-03-04 04:31] http404error: It is Prompt, which is mutually exclusive with "buildtime" conditions
[2018-03-04 04:32] http404error: It isn't clickable until the next turn anyways
[2018-03-04 04:32] http404error: As we just talked about for a few minutes straight
[2018-03-04 04:33] strshplyr5046 (StrShPlyr): Prompt would mean Blocks right before built.
[2018-03-04 04:35] http404error: I think you're missing some critical pieces of understanding, probably because you haven't played the game.
[2018-03-04 04:35] http404error: Because everything I'm saying is sensible and correct, to everyone but you.
[2018-03-04 04:35] http404error: Don't complain about your ignorance.

[2018-03-05 09:11] saracen26: That was a fascinating insight. How something as simple, yet complex as SDR effectively governs the design and most of the game's mechanics is incredible.
[2018-03-05 09:19] mqp: i think the article doesn't say the biggest reason i think shalev's rules are roughly good ideas, which is that they provide focus for what you need to reason about during your time in the game
[2018-03-05 09:21] mqp: since prismata remains basically always all about who has more attack per turn, more HP, and more economy in some combination, the game is about figuring which combination in which order is best and then figuring out the tactically optimal way to get there
[2018-03-05 09:21] mqp: and you can do a lot of local tactical reasoning by looking ahead a small distance, observing that one situation is strictly superior (or near-strictly superior) to another one, and trimming your tree of variations
[2018-03-05 09:23] mqp: if it wasn't true that having more resources and more attack and more HP was usually better, then your position evaluation would get a lot more complicated and you would have to work out a lot more variations
[2018-03-05 09:23] mqp: probably it would feel like you wanted longer time controls

[2018-03-06 00:35] .jrkirby (jrkirby): Game mode concept:

Prismata Drafting Gauntlet

Play through a gauntlet of 7 games in a row, while drafting tools to help you beat your opponents. Each game, you are matched against someone who is equally far into the gauntlet. At the start of each game, you and your opponent draft units to help you along your way. You are presented with three options to choose from, and you each see what the other player has drafted so far, and the options they have right now. Most games, you are given unit blueprints, but before games 3 and 6 you get to draft cheap units that start on your side of the battlefield at the start of each game. During the game, you can only build the units you have drafted, and the base set. Your units are displayed at the top of the advanced set, your opponents at the bottom.

Draft wisely, considering both synergies between what you have so far, and counters to the build that your opponent has so far. A combination of both luck and skill are needed to prevail to the end, as only 1 in 128 runs will succeed!

Obviously, the current playerbase is not large enough for such a game-mode to work currently, as player with 6 wins would be waiting around forever to find a match even at peak playtimes. But if the playerbase expanded greatly, I think it'd be an immensely fun casual format to prismata, where luck and skill would both combine to allow for exciting, unique, and interesting situations.
